metadata.description: A 40-question psychometric test across 4 sections that maps student responses to career-aligned specialization traits.

scoring.explanation: Each option maps to one or more traits with a weight (1–3). Final score per specialization = sum of weights of matched traits. Higher score = better fit.

sections.description: What genuinely excites you? There are no right or wrong answers.

questions.question: When you have free time and access to a computer, what are you most likely to do?

options.text: Write code, build a small project or debug something
Design a UI mockup, edit graphics or create digital art
Read about AI research, watch ML tutorials or experiment with models
Read news, browse social media trends or write something

questions.question: Which subject in school do / did you enjoy the most?

options.text: Mathematics — I love solving equations and proofs
Physics — circuits, mechanics, and how things work
Biology / Chemistry — the science of life and matter
Economics / Commerce — markets, money, and business
English / Arts — language, literature, and expression

questions.question: Pick the project you'd most enjoy working on for a year:

options.text: Building an app that millions of people use every day
Designing and building a physical structure — a bridge, building, or machine
Researching a cure for a disease or a new scientific theory
Launching and growing a business from scratch
Investigating a social issue and writing stories that drive change

questions.question: Which of these YouTube rabbit holes would you fall into at 2 AM?

options.text: How neural networks actually learn — visualizations and explainers
How electronics / CPUs are made — chip fabrication and engineering
How massive dams, skyscrapers, or roads are built
UI/UX redesigns, design critiques, or colour theory deep-dives
True crime documentaries or investigative journalism breakdowns

questions.question: Which of these roles sounds most exciting to you?

options.text: Lawyer arguing a high-stakes corporate case
Doctor diagnosing rare diseases and helping patients
Data Scientist finding hidden patterns in huge datasets
Financial Analyst managing investments for a firm
Journalist breaking a major news story

questions.question: Which type of work environment sounds most appealing?

options.text: A quiet lab or research center where I can focus deeply
A busy site — field, factory, hospital or construction zone
A fast-paced office or startup where things change daily
Anywhere — I want to work remotely on my laptop
A courtroom, studio, or public-facing setting

questions.question: What kind of problem do you most enjoy solving?

options.text: A logical puzzle or algorithm challenge
A design challenge — making something look and feel perfect
A numbers problem — analysis, statistics, or financial modelling
A human problem — understanding why people behave the way they do
An engineering problem — optimizing a physical or electrical system

questions.question: How do you usually spend your school/college fest time?

options.text: Competing in coding, quiz or debate events
Performing, anchoring or running the media/coverage team
Organising logistics, managing teams or sponsorships
Designing posters, making videos or working on the website
Attending workshops and exploring new technical things

questions.question: If you could shadow a professional for a week, who would you pick?

options.text: A structural or civil engineer working on a mega infrastructure project
A product manager at a top tech company (Google, Amazon)
An AI researcher publishing papers at NeurIPS
A surgeon performing complex operations
A stock trader or investment banker managing millions

questions.question: Which of these clubs would you most likely join?

options.text: Robotics or Electronics Club
Entrepreneurship or Finance Club
Photography, Filmmaking or Journalism Club
Competitive Programming or AI/ML Club
Social Work / NSS / Community Service

sections.description: How do you naturally think and process information?

questions.question: A function is supposed to return the second largest number in a list, but it's returning the wrong answer. How do you approach this?

options.text: I trace through the logic step by step and find the exact line that's wrong
I Google the error, find a Stack Overflow post and adapt the solution
I rewrite the function from scratch with a cleaner approach
This scenario doesn't interest me — I'd move on to something else

questions.question: You're given 3 years of sales data for a company. What's your first instinct?

options.text: Plot the data and look for patterns, seasonality or anomalies
Calculate year-on-year growth, margins and forecast future revenue
Ask what decision needs to be made and what information actually matters
Turn it into a visual story — a report or presentation

questions.question: When you study a new concept, which approach works best for you?

options.text: Read the theory thoroughly, then attempt problems
Jump straight in and learn by trial and error
Watch a visual explanation or diagram first, then connect the dots
Discuss it with someone and build understanding through conversation

questions.question: Which task would you complete faster and more accurately?

options.text: Spotting an error in a 500-line spreadsheet with financial data
Drawing a floor plan or visualizing how a space could be redesigned
Reading a legal contract and identifying the problematic clauses
Dissecting a mechanical or electronic device to understand how it works

questions.question: How do you typically handle a complex, multi-week project?

options.text: Break it into sub-tasks, set milestones, and track progress daily
Sprint hard when inspired, then review everything at the end
Coordinate with others constantly and divide work by strengths
Research first, experiment a lot, then commit to the best approach

questions.question: You need to understand how a power grid distributes electricity across a city. How do you think about it?

options.text: I think of it as a graph or network — nodes, edges, load balancing
I think of transformers, voltage levels and circuit components
I'd want to see the policy side — who regulates it and how pricing works
This doesn't really interest me

questions.question: Someone asks you to explain a complex topic (e.g. how the internet works) to a 12-year-old. What do you do?

options.text: Use an analogy — compare it to something they already know
Draw a diagram or sketch it out visually
Walk them through it step-by-step with examples
Give them a hands-on activity so they can discover it themselves

questions.question: You've noticed a pattern: every time it rains, your city's traffic app crashes. What do you do?

options.text: Try to replicate the bug, isolate the cause and fix the code
Collect data on both — rainfall, traffic load and crash logs — and correlate
Report it as a story or public interest issue
Design a better UX / fallback screen so users aren't confused

questions.question: Which of these is closest to how you make decisions?

options.text: I analyze all options with data and logic before deciding
I go with my gut — fast decisions, course-correct later
I consult people I trust, hear multiple perspectives, then decide
I follow established rules or frameworks — consistency matters

questions.question: A startup asks you to join. Which role would you naturally gravitate towards?

options.text: Lead engineer — architect the backend systems
Product/UX lead — own the user experience end to end
Finance/ops lead — manage budgets, unit economics, hiring
Marketing/comms lead — build the brand and audience

sections.description: How you naturally show up in life and work.

questions.question: Your friends would describe you as someone who…

options.text: Always has a fix for anything technical or logical
Is incredibly creative and has great aesthetic taste
Stays calm and thinks clearly even under pressure
Is very organized and gets things done on time, every time
Lights up any room and makes everyone feel heard

questions.question: How do you feel after spending 3 hours alone working deeply on something?

options.text: Energized and satisfied — deep focus is my best state
Accomplished but ready to share and get feedback
Drained — I do my best work in short bursts with breaks
It depends — if I was solving something hard, great; if tedious, awful

questions.question: When you're part of a group project, which role do you naturally take?

options.text: The one who takes charge and divides up the work
The one who quietly does the most technical/difficult work
The one who makes sure everyone is on the same page
The one who comes up with the creative ideas and concepts

questions.question: How do you feel about following strict rules or standard procedures?

options.text: I like them — they bring clarity and prevent mistakes
I follow them but always look for ways to improve the process
I prefer flexibility — I'll find my own best way to the result
Rules are necessary in some contexts (medicine/law) but I hate bureaucracy

questions.question: How comfortable are you with uncertainty and ambiguity?

options.text: Very comfortable — ambiguity is where creativity lives
Somewhat — I'm okay with it if I have a clear goal
Not very — I prefer clear tasks with defined outcomes
I thrive under pressure but need some data to guide me

questions.question: A friend comes to you in an emotional crisis at midnight. You…

options.text: Drop everything and listen without judgment until they feel better
Listen, then immediately try to help them solve the problem
Ask what they need — listening ear, advice, or distraction — and do that
This makes me uncomfortable — I struggle with emotional situations

questions.question: How do you typically react to a failure or a mistake you made?

options.text: I analyse what went wrong, document it, and make sure it doesn't repeat
I feel bad briefly, then pivot and try a completely different approach
I talk it through with someone to gain perspective
I stay calm and methodically check where the system/process broke down

questions.question: You're asked to give a presentation to 200 people next week. You feel…

options.text: Excited — I love being on stage and commanding an audience
Okay — I'll prepare thoroughly and deliver it well
Nervous but I'll manage — public speaking isn't natural for me
I'd rather send a detailed written report instead

questions.question: Which of these best describes how you spend weekends?

options.text: Building or tinkering with something — hardware, code, or crafts
Going out, meeting people, attending events
Reading, researching or consuming content I find interesting
Outdoor activities — trekking, sports, travel

questions.question: Which of these statements feels most true about you?

options.text: I notice details that most people miss
I naturally see the big picture and long-term patterns
I connect with people quickly and understand what they need
I learn new things very fast and adapt to any situation

sections.description: What matters to you most in life and career?

questions.question: What's your biggest career goal?

options.text: Build technology that changes how people live
Earn high and build financial independence early
Help people directly — as a doctor, lawyer or social worker
Make my mark creatively — in design, media, or storytelling
Build critical infrastructure — power systems, roads, systems that last

questions.question: Which of these means more to you?

options.text: Being the smartest person in the room (depth of knowledge)
Being the most connected person in the room (breadth of relationships)
Being the person who gets things done (execution)
Being the most creative person in the room (originality)

questions.question: How important is job security to you?

options.text: Very important — a government job or stable career is my priority
Moderately — I want stability but I'm open to opportunity
Not much — I'd rather take risks for bigger rewards
I want to build my own business — employment is the safety net, not the goal

questions.question: Which of these impacts feels most meaningful to you?

options.text: Writing a research paper that advances human knowledge
Designing a product used by millions of people
Treating 50 patients a day and improving their health
Breaking a news story that changes public policy
Building infrastructure that a city uses for 100 years

questions.question: Which value resonates with you most strongly?

options.text: Precision and accuracy — doing things right the first time
Curiosity and learning — constantly growing and discovering
Connection and community — building strong relationships
Impact and scale — making a big difference in the world

questions.question: What do you want people to say about you at the end of your career?

options.text: "She/He was the best engineer/scientist in the field"
"She/He built systems that millions of people rely on"
"She/He truly cared about people and made their lives better"
"She/He was a visionary — ahead of their time"
"She/He told stories that changed how people think"

questions.question: Which trade-off would you make?

options.text: Lower salary but work that genuinely helps people
Higher stress but cutting-edge, exciting work
Stable income doing predictable, reliable work
High earnings doing financially complex work

questions.question: Which statement best reflects your relationship with technology?

options.text: I want to build technology — I think in code and systems
I use technology as a creative medium — design, content, media
Technology is a tool — useful but not my passion
I'm interested in the hardware and electronics side of technology

questions.question: In 10 years, where do you see yourself?

options.text: Running my own company or leading a major team
As a deep domain expert — the go-to person in my field
In a role where I interact with and help people every day
Doing creative work that I'm proud of

questions.question: What would make you quit a job that pays well?

options.text: The work is ethically wrong or harms people
There's nothing to learn — I've stopped growing
The work is too repetitive — I need creative challenge
I'm not making enough impact or building enough depth

scoring_engine.description: How to compute specialization scores from responses
steps: 1. For each answer selected, retrieve its traits array.
2. For each trait in the array, add its weight to that trait's running total.
3. After all 40 questions, you have a trait_scores dict: { trait_name: total_weight }
4. For each specialization, compute match_score = sum of trait_scores[t] for each trait t in specialization.psychometric_traits (if t exists in trait_scores).

5. Normalize: divide each specialization's match_score by its maximum possible score (= 3 * number_of_traits, since max weight per trait = 3 per question * up to 10 relevant questions). In practice, normalize by the highest score among all specializations.
6. Sort specializations by normalized match_score descending.
7. Return top 3 specializations as recommendations with match percentage.
scoring_engine.tie_breaking: If two specializations have the same score, prefer the one whose stream matches the student's declared stream (science/commerce/independent).
scoring_engine.minimum_threshold_note: Specializations below 20% of max possible score should not be recommended.
